summary: Jeff Atwood documents his first PC upgrade in 5 years, shifting focus from raw performance gains to building powerful systems in smaller form factors. He compares several mini-ITX cases, ultimately choosing the Streacom DA2 for its balance of size and cooling capacity. The post details his high-end build specs and offers practical advice on case configuration, airflow optimization, and the tradeoffs of cramming powerful components into compact spaces. He concludes that miniaturization, not speed, represents the primary innovation in PC building for 2020 and beyond.
key_points: PC upgrades have become boring enough that 5 years passed between builds, reinforcing the idea that the PC era is waning
The future of PC building is shifting from more speed to smaller form factors that maintain full power
The DAN A4 SFX at 7.3 liters may be the smallest possible standard mini-ITX build, but the larger DA2 offers better cooling for high-end components
Removing the dust mesh panels for open-air cooling dramatically improved temperatures in the DA2
Modern PC building has become remarkably simple - installing M.2 drives, memory, CPU, and cooler on the motherboard gets you most of the way done
money_quotes: It took 5 years for me to muster up the initiative to get my system fully upgraded!
I guess that's yet another sign that the PC is over, because PC upgrades have gotten really boring.
The future of PCs may not necessarily be more speed, but in smaller builds.
The attraction here is maximum power in minimum size.
About 50% more volume for considerably more than 2× the power isn't a bad deal!
I had dramatically better temperatures after I did this, and it also made the build easier since the case can fully 'breathe' through the top and bottom.
If you haven't built a PC system recently, it's easier than it has ever been.
Perhaps that's the primary innovation in PC building for 2020 and beyond – packing all that power into less than 20 liters of space!
key_insight: As raw PC performance gains have plateaued in relevance, the new frontier of enthusiast PC building has become achieving maximum power in minimum physical space.
